planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, fertile, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil in partial shade to full sun in zone 8–11; Zedoary is a large, clumping tropical ginger native to northeast India and the Indo-Malayan rainforest zone, grown for its camphor-pepper-bitter aromatic rhizomes used in Southeast Asian and South Asian cuisine, traditional medicine, and perfumery — historically traded on the medieval European spice routes.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions 3–4 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade; Curcuma zedoaria is dormant in winter — cut back dead stems after frost and mulch heavily in zone 8–9, then lift and store rhizomes in colder climates. Curcuma zedoaria (Zedoary or White Turmeric) is a large, ornamental, clumping ginger-family perennial native to northeast India (Assam), Bangladesh, and the Malay Archipelago, cultivated throughout South and Southeast Asia for its fat, fleshy, grayish-white rhizomes with a distinctive camphor-lemon-pepper-bitter aroma and flavor profile entirely different from common turmeric (C. longa). The rhizomes are used fresh, dried and ground, or steam-distilled for essential oil in Indonesian, Thai, and Indian cuisines for curries, pickles, and medicinal preparations; zedoary was also an important European medieval spice — traded under the name 'zedoary' in Arab and European commerce as early as the 6th century CE. Medicinally, the rhizomes are used in Ayurvedic and Unani medicine as a digestive tonic, carminative, and anti-inflammatory. The plant is also ornamentally attractive — large, paddle-shaped leaves with a distinctive purple midrib stripe, and showy, cone-shaped pink-and-white flower spikes emerge from the ground before the main foliage in spring. Excellent food garden spice for partial-shade positions in zones 8–11.
sources: Kew Science Plants of the World — Curcuma zedoaria
Indian Council of Agricultural Research — Zedoary

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions 3–4 inches deep in spring in moist, fertile, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil in partial shade in zone 8–11; Mango Ginger is a distinctive Indian Curcuma species prized for rhizomes with an unmistakable raw mango (green mango / Mangifera indica) aroma and flavor — without any turmeric or ginger heat — used fresh in South Indian pickles, chutneys, and salads as a seasonal delicacy.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions 3–4 inches deep in moist, fertile, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; Curcuma amada is dormant in winter — cut back dead foliage after frost, mulch the root zone heavily in zone 8–9 to protect against freezing, and replant or divide in spring. Curcuma amada (Mango Ginger or Amba Haldi) is a clumping, rhizomatous ginger-family perennial native to subtropical India (West Bengal, Odisha, northeast India) and cultivated throughout South Asia for its plump, whitish to pale-yellow rhizomes — valued for the extraordinary raw green mango scent and flavor profile unique to this species within the Curcuma genus, with no heat or turmeric bitterness. The fresh rhizomes are sliced, pickled, or grated in South Indian and Sri Lankan seasonal preparations — particularly the South Indian festival pickle ambahaladi or amada pachadi — and are sold fresh at South Asian markets during the brief harvest season (October–January). The flavor is genuinely mango-like, mild, slightly sour, and entirely distinct from all other gingers. Mango Ginger is an excellent culinary novelty for partial-shade herb gardens in zone 8–11, dying back completely in winter and re-emerging reliably from its persistent rhizome in spring. The inflorescences are also ornamentally attractive: pale-pink, cone-shaped spikes that emerge before the foliage in spring.
sources: Indian Council of Agricultural Research — Curcuma amada
Kew Science Plants of the World — Curcuma amada

subcategory: South Asian cosmetic and medicinal rhizome

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions 3–4 inches deep in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade in zone 8–11; Wild Turmeric is a showy ornamental and cosmetic/medicinal Curcuma native to the eastern Himalayas and South Asia, grown for deeply yellow rhizomes with a rose-camphor-turmeric aroma used in skin-lightening and cosmetic preparations across South Asia — less culinary than common turmeric and famous for producing some of the most spectacular pink flower spikes of the genus.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade; Curcuma aromatica is dormant in winter and emerges in spring — mulch heavily to protect rhizomes from frost below zone 9, lift and store indoors in colder climates. Curcuma aromatica (Wild Turmeric, Kasturi Manjal, or Cochin Haldi) is a clumping rhizomatous ginger-family perennial native to the subtropical forests of northeast India, Bhutan, and Myanmar, now cultivated across South Asia, Southeast Asia, and China. The rhizomes are bright orange-yellow internally — richly aromatic with a floral rose-camphor-turmeric scent — and are the primary ingredient in traditional South Asian skin-care and cosmetic preparations: traditional face packs (ubtan), skin-brightening pastes, and bath scrubs used in Hindu ritual bathing ceremonies, wedding preparations, and daily skin care, particularly in Kerala, Tamil Nadu, and West Bengal. The curcumin content is lower than common turmeric (C. longa) so it is rarely used as a food spice. The ornamental value is exceptional: stunning pink to rose-purple, cone-shaped flower spikes emerge directly from the ground on separate stems before the large paddle-shaped leaves in spring — among the most beautiful of all Curcuma flowers. Excellent partial-shade ornamental spice plant for zone 8–11 gardens.
sources: Kew Science Plants of the World — Curcuma aromatica

planting.note: Divide clumps or plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade in zone 8–11; Yi Zhi (Sharpleaf Galangal) is a clumping ginger-family perennial native to Hainan Island and southeastern China, commercially cultivated on Hainan for its aromatic, peppery, warmly spiced seed pods — among the most highly valued medicinal herbs in traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) for treating cold conditions of the spleen and kidney.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade; Alpinia oxyphylla prefers high humidity and consistent moisture — mulch deeply, water regularly during the growing season, and cut back dead stems after dormancy; mulch root zone in zone 8 to protect from hard freezes. Alpinia oxyphylla (Yi Zhi or Sharpleaf Galangal) is a large, clumping, rhizomatous ginger-family perennial native to the tropical forest understory of Hainan Island, Guangdong, and Guangxi provinces in southern China. It is one of the 'Four Southern Chinese Herbs' (Nan Yao Sì Hua) — Hainan-native plants of highest importance in Traditional Chinese Medicine — commercially cultivated at scale on Hainan as one of the most economically significant medicinal crops of the island. The aromatic, cone-shaped seed pods (harvested before full ripeness and dried) are used in TCM preparations for digestive complaints, frequent urination, and cold-deficiency conditions of the spleen and kidney. The spicy, warming, aromatic seeds taste of a combination of cardamom, pepper, and ginger. The plant is also ornamentally beautiful — tall, graceful, lanceolate leaves and elegant, arching inflorescences bearing white flowers with bright-pink suffused lips. Excellent partial-shade specimen for Southern gardens (zone 8–11) where the tall, tropical-looking foliage provides substantial presence even when not in bloom.
sources: Hainan Medicinal Plants Research Institute — Alpinia oxyphylla

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring or plant container stock in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil in partial shade in zone 9–12; Red Spiral Ginger is a striking, clumping tropical perennial from Costa Rica and Central America bearing spirally arranged leaves on upright canes and producing brilliant scarlet-red, cone-shaped terminal bracts from which small, yellow, tubular flowers emerge — providing months of colorful display in warm, humid gardens.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to bright indirect light; Costus barbatus performs best in high humidity with consistent moisture — mulch heavily, water regularly in summer, and protect from frost; in zone 9 mulch heavily and cut back frost-damaged canes. Costus barbatus (Red Spiral Ginger or Scarlet Spiral Flag) is a clumping, cane-forming, rhizomatous perennial in the Costaceae family — close relatives of the gingers — native to the humid forests and forest edges of Costa Rica, Panama, and adjacent Central America. The hallmark of the plant is its beautiful stem architecture: the broad, lanceolate leaves are arranged in a perfect ascending spiral around each upright cane, giving the plant an elegant, architectural quality even without flowers. The flower heads are spectacular: large, cone-shaped, brilliant scarlet-red bracts tightly overlapping at the top of each cane, from which small, yellow, tubular flowers emerge one or a few at a time over several months — the red bracts persist long after the flowers, extending the display. Hummingbirds work the flowers intensively. Costus barbatus thrives in partial shade, making it an outstanding choice for shadier positions in tropical and subtropical gardens where few other plants produce such vibrant color. Divide clumps every 2–3 years to maintain vigor. Excellent for container growing in bright, humid interiors in cooler climates.
sources: Smithsonian Tropical Research Institute — Costus barbatus
University of Florida IFAS — Ornamental Gingers for Florida
Marie Selby Botanical Gardens — Costaceae Collection

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions 2–3 inches deep in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade in zone 8–11; Cassumunar Ginger (Plai) is a medium-sized ginger-family perennial native to Thailand, India, and Southeast Asia grown for its aromatic rhizomes with an intense green-pine-turpentine aroma — the source of plai essential oil widely used in Thai traditional massage therapy, pain-relief liniments, and deep-tissue anti-inflammatory preparations.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist, fertile, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade; Zingiber montanum dies back in winter in zone 8–9 — mulch the root zone heavily after frost and the plant will re-emerge reliably from the persistent rhizome in late spring. Zingiber montanum (Cassumunar Ginger, Plai, or Bengal Root) is a medium-sized rhizomatous ginger-family perennial native to South Asia (India, Bangladesh) and Southeast Asia (Thailand, Myanmar, Laos, Vietnam, Indonesia), where its aromatic, yellow-fleshed rhizomes are the source of one of the most important herbal medicines in Thai traditional medicine — 'Plai' or 'Phlai.' The essential oil distilled from the rhizomes is rich in terpinen-4-ol and sabinene, giving it strong anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and antispasmodic properties extensively documented in Thai traditional medicine for joint pain, sprains, bruises, and postpartum care. Plai oil is a standard ingredient in Thai massage therapy liniments and is now internationally distributed as a sports and pain-relief product. The plant is ornamentally neat — compact, clumping, with attractive narrow lanceolate leaves — and the aromatic rhizomes are easy to harvest by simply digging, taking what is needed, and replanting the remainder. An outstanding and under-grown medicinal spice for warm-climate herb gardens in zone 8–11.
sources: Thailand Department of Medical Sciences — Zingiber montanum (Plai)
